Scoring_Rationale: Common backend-grid integration and practical command injection could affect fleet load and grid stability.

Threat_Scenario: Manipulated demand-response or DER linkage data
Scoring_Rationale: Grid-responsive charging is widely deployed and manipulated control data can affect site or feeder load.
Device-model telemetry is common but reachability and safety impact depend on charger composition and deployment.
The path exists in certificate workflows but is commonly isolated behind a registration-authority or PKI service boundary.

Threat_Scenario: Control-message injection through the charger-internal integration path
Scoring_Rationale: The two functions often coexist in a charger while their internal boundary is outside both protocol standards.

Threat_Scenario: Man-in-the-middle on wireless backhaul or field access
Scoring_Rationale: Wireless and cellular access are common and expose operational traffic when medium-specific protections are weak or misconfigured.

Threat_Scenario: Manipulated grid-linkage commands for internal power components
Scoring_Rationale: BESS and power-module grid functions are credible but the direct interface depends on site architecture.

Threat_Scenario: Abuse of independent wireless access to internal components
Scoring_Rationale: Independent modems and maintenance radios are deployment-dependent but can expose safety-relevant components.

Threat_Scenario: Abuse of telematics or in-vehicle wireless interfaces
Scoring_Rationale: Telematics access is common but compromise of charging-relevant internal functions requires additional vehicle-specific steps.

Threat_Scenario: Abuse of charger certificate issuance or management
Scoring_Rationale: OCPP defines certificate enrollment through backend brokering but not a direct charger-to-CA security edge.

Threat_Scenario: Manipulated payment roaming or external-service data
Scoring_Rationale: Payment and roaming exchanges are common and confidentiality impact is high while direct grid impact is limited.

Threat_Scenario: Abuse of an internal-device certificate or security-module path
Scoring_Rationale: Internal metering or security modules may use certificates but topology and network reachability are product-specific.

Threat_Scenario: Abuse of payment-terminal or settlement linkage
Scoring_Rationale: External payment connectivity is credible but the internal-component boundary varies by charger design.
Direct EVCC-grid exchange is uncommon but bidirectional-power use cases give integrity failures a wide operational scope.

Threat_Scenario: Abuse of a vehicle security-module to PKI path
Scoring_Rationale: Certificate handling is usually mediated by EVCC or OEM services but compromise can undermine vehicle trust credentials.

Threat_Scenario: Manipulated vehicle-internal power-state or DER data
Scoring_Rationale: An independent direct edge is exceptional and normally mediated by EVCC EVSE or backend systems.
The use case is conceivable in smart charging but SECC is rarely an independent utility endpoint.
Direct operational exchange depends on the business role and is often mediated by an aggregator or smart-charging provider.

Threat_Scenario: Confusion or compromise across grid-side and V2G trust infrastructures
Scoring_Rationale: Grid authentication infrastructure exists but a direct link to the V2G PKI is architecture-specific.
